kids.question: What does James say we should feel when we face troubles?
kids.explanation: James 1:2 — "My brethren, count it all joy when ye fall into divers temptations."

kids.question: If you need wisdom, what should you do?
kids.explanation: James 1:5 — "If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not."

kids.question: James says we should not just hear God's word but also do what?
kids.explanation: James 1:22 — "But be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving your own selves."

teens.question: What does the trying of your faith produce?
teens.option_a: Patience
teens.explanation: James 1:3 — "Knowing this, that the trying of your faith worketh patience."

teens.question: What is a double minded man compared to?
teens.option_a: A wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed
teens.explanation: James 1:6 — "He that wavereth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed."

teens.question: What does lust bring forth when it is finished?
teens.option_a: Sin, and sin when finished bringeth forth death
teens.explanation: James 1:15 — "Then when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin: and sin, when it is finished, bringeth forth death."

teens.question: What three things does James say every man should be?
teens.option_a: Swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath
teens.option_b: Slow to hear, swift to speak, quick to anger
teens.option_c: Quick to judge, slow to forgive, fast to act
teens.option_d: First to speak, last to listen, quick to fight
teens.explanation: James 1:19 — "Let every man be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath."

teens.question: What is pure religion before God the Father?
teens.option_a: To visit the fatherless and widows, and keep himself unspotted from the world
teens.option_c: To give all your money away
teens.option_d: To fast and pray at all times
teens.explanation: James 1:27 — "Pure religion and undefiled before God and the Father is this, To visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction, and to keep himself unspotted from the world."

adults.question: Who does James identify himself as and who does he address?
adults.option_a: A servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, to the twelve tribes scattered abroad
adults.option_b: The brother of Jesus, to the church in Jerusalem
adults.option_c: An apostle of Christ, to the Gentiles
adults.option_d: A prophet of Israel, to the Jewish leaders
adults.explanation: James 1:1 — "James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, to the twelve tribes which are scattered abroad, greeting."

adults.question: What does James say about God and temptation in verse 13?
adults.option_a: God cannot be tempted with evil, neither tempteth he any man
adults.option_b: God tempts people to test their strength
adults.option_c: God allows temptation only for the righteous
adults.option_d: God and evil are in constant battle over temptation
adults.explanation: James 1:13 — "Let no man say when he is tempted, I am tempted of God: for God cannot be tempted with evil, neither tempteth he any man."

adults.question: What does James say about every good and perfect gift?
adults.option_a: It comes down from the Father of lights, with whom is no variableness, neither shadow of turning
adults.option_b: It comes from human effort and hard work
adults.option_c: It is earned through religious rituals
adults.option_d: It comes from the angels who serve God
adults.explanation: James 1:17 — "Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and cometh down from the Father of lights, with whom is no variableness, neither shadow of turning."

adults.question: What does James call believers in verse 18?
adults.option_a: A kind of firstfruits of his creatures
adults.option_b: The final harvest of God's plan
adults.option_c: The remnant of Israel
adults.option_d: The chosen elite of heaven
adults.explanation: James 1:18 — "Of his own will begat he us with the word of truth, that we should be a kind of firstfruits of his creatures."

adults.question: What does James compare the hearer who is not a doer to?
adults.option_a: A man beholding his natural face in a glass who goes away and straightway forgetteth what manner of man he was
adults.option_b: A wise builder who lays a strong foundation
adults.option_c: A shepherd who tends his sheep carefully
adults.option_d: A farmer who plants and waters diligently
adults.explanation: James 1:23-24 — "He is like unto a man beholding his natural face in a glass: For he beholdeth himself, and goeth his way, and straightway forgetteth what manner of man he was."

adults.question: What does James call the law that brings blessing to the doer?
adults.option_a: The perfect law of liberty
adults.option_b: The law of Moses
adults.option_c: The law of the prophets
adults.option_d: The law of circumcision
adults.explanation: James 1:25 — "But whoso looketh into the perfect law of liberty, and continueth therein, he being not a forgetful hearer, but a doer of the work, this man shall be blessed in his deed."

adults.question: What does James say about the low brother and the rich in verses 9-10?
adults.option_a: The brother of low degree should rejoice that he is exalted; the rich that he is made low
adults.option_b: The low should envy the rich; the rich should boast
adults.option_c: The low should serve the rich; the rich should command
adults.option_d: The low should hide; the rich should display their glory
adults.explanation: James 1:9-10 — "Let the brother of low degree rejoice in that he is exalted: But the rich, in that he is made low: because as the flower of the grass he shall pass away."

adults.question: What crown is promised to the man who endures temptation?
adults.option_a: The crown of life, which the Lord hath promised to them that love him
adults.option_b: The crown of thorns shared with Christ
adults.option_c: The crown of righteousness for all believers
adults.option_d: The crown of gold for the wealthy faithful
adults.explanation: James 1:12 — "Blessed is the man that endureth temptation: for when he is tried, he shall receive the crown of life, which the Lord hath promised to them that love him."

kids.question: If a rich man and a poor man come to church, how should you treat them?
kids.option_a: Tell the poor man to leave
kids.option_b: Give the rich man the best seat
kids.option_d: Only talk to the rich man
kids.explanation: James 2:1-4 — James warns against showing favouritism, telling the poor man "Stand thou there" while giving the rich man a good place.

kids.question: What does James call the commandment "Love thy neighbour as thyself"?
kids.explanation: James 2:8 — "If ye fulfil the royal law according to the scripture, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself, ye do well."

kids.question: What does James say about faith without doing good deeds?
kids.explanation: James 2:26 — "For as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead also."

teens.question: What does James say about showing respect of persons (favouritism)?
teens.option_a: It is sin and transgression of the law
teens.explanation: James 2:9 — "But if ye have respect to persons, ye commit sin, and are convinced of the law as transgressors."

teens.question: What happens if you keep the whole law but offend in one point?
teens.option_a: You are guilty of all
teens.option_d: That one point does not count
teens.explanation: James 2:10 — "For whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend in one point, he is guilty of all."

teens.question: Who is the Old Testament example James uses for faith shown by works?
teens.option_a: Abraham, when he offered Isaac his son upon the altar
teens.option_b: Moses, when he parted the Red Sea
teens.option_d: Elijah, when he called down fire
teens.explanation: James 2:21 — "Was not Abraham our father justified by works, when he had offered Isaac his son upon the altar?"

teens.question: What other person besides Abraham showed faith by works?
teens.option_a: Rahab the harlot, when she received the messengers
teens.option_c: Esther, when she saved her people
teens.explanation: James 2:25 — "Likewise also was not Rahab the harlot justified by works, when she had received the messengers, and had sent them out another way?"

teens.question: What does James compare faith without works to?
teens.option_a: A body without the spirit
teens.explanation: James 2:26 — "For as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead also."

adults.question: How does James describe the treatment of the poor man in the assembly?
adults.option_a: Told to stand there or sit under the footstool while the rich man gets the good place
adults.option_b: Given food and drink before the rich man
adults.option_c: Asked to lead the congregation in prayer
adults.option_d: Welcomed warmly by all the members
adults.explanation: James 2:3 — "And ye say to the poor, Stand thou there, or sit here under my footstool."

adults.question: What did God choose the poor of this world to be?
adults.option_a: Rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom which he hath promised to them that love him
adults.option_b: Leaders of the synagogues
adults.option_c: Missionaries to foreign lands
adults.option_d: Temple servants in Jerusalem
adults.explanation: James 2:5 — "Hath not God chosen the poor of this world rich in faith, and heirs of the kingdom which he hath promised to them that love him?"

adults.question: What does James say the rich do to believers?
adults.option_a: They oppress you and draw you before the judgment seats
adults.option_b: They help and support the church
adults.option_c: They build synagogues for believers
adults.option_d: They send gifts to the apostles
adults.explanation: James 2:6 — "Do not rich men oppress you, and draw you before the judgment seats?"

adults.question: What does James say about mercy and judgment?
adults.option_a: Mercy rejoiceth against judgment
adults.option_b: Judgment always triumphs over mercy
adults.option_c: Mercy and judgment are equal
adults.option_d: Judgment is unnecessary where mercy exists
adults.explanation: James 2:13 — "For he shall have judgment without mercy, that hath shewed no mercy; and mercy rejoiceth against judgment."

adults.question: What illustration shows that faith without works is dead?
adults.option_a: Saying to a naked and hungry brother "be ye warmed and filled" without giving them things needful
adults.option_b: Building a house on a strong foundation
adults.option_c: Planting a vineyard and tending it carefully
adults.option_d: Lighting a candle and placing it on a candlestick
adults.explanation: James 2:15-16 — "If a brother or sister be naked, and destitute of daily food, And one of you say unto them, Depart in peace, be ye warmed and filled; notwithstanding ye give them not those things which are needful to the body; what doth it profit?"

adults.question: What does James say the devils do regarding belief in God?
adults.option_a: The devils also believe and tremble
adults.option_b: The devils deny God exists
adults.option_c: The devils worship God willingly
adults.option_d: The devils are indifferent to God
adults.explanation: James 2:19 — "Thou believest that there is one God; thou doest well: the devils also believe, and tremble."

adults.question: How does James describe the relationship between faith and works in Abraham?
adults.option_a: Faith wrought with his works, and by works was faith made perfect
adults.option_b: Faith replaced his works entirely
adults.option_c: Works were unnecessary alongside his faith
adults.option_d: Faith and works were in conflict
adults.explanation: James 2:22 — "Seest thou how faith wrought with his works, and by works was faith made perfect?"

adults.question: What was Abraham called because of his faith?
adults.option_a: The Friend of God
adults.option_b: The Father of Nations
adults.option_c: The Patriarch of Israel
adults.option_d: The Servant of the Lord
adults.explanation: James 2:23 — "And the scripture was fulfilled which saith, Abraham believed God, and it was imputed unto him for righteousness: and he was called the Friend of God."

kids.question: What small body part can cause big trouble?
kids.explanation: James 3:5-6 — "Even so the tongue is a little member, and boasteth great things. Behold, how great a matter a little fire kindleth!"

kids.question: What does James compare the tongue to?
kids.explanation: James 3:6 — "And the tongue is a fire, a world of iniquity."

kids.question: Can a fountain send out both sweet and bitter water?
kids.explanation: James 3:11 — "Doth a fountain send forth at the same place sweet water and bitter?"

teens.question: Why does James warn against many becoming masters (teachers)?
teens.option_a: Because they shall receive the greater condemnation
teens.option_d: Because there are already too many teachers
teens.explanation: James 3:1 — "My brethren, be not many masters, knowing that we shall receive the greater condemnation."

teens.question: What two small things does James say control bigger things, like the tongue?
teens.option_a: A bit in a horse's mouth and a small helm (rudder) on a ship
teens.option_b: A key in a lock and a button on a coat
teens.option_c: A seed in the ground and a drop in the ocean
teens.option_d: A needle in a haystack and a star in the sky
teens.explanation: James 3:3-4 — "We put bits in the horses' mouths... Behold also the ships, which though they be so great... are turned about with a very small helm."

teens.question: What does James say no man can tame?
teens.option_a: The tongue
teens.explanation: James 3:8 — "But the tongue can no man tame; it is an unruly evil, full of deadly poison."

teens.question: What is the tongue full of according to James?
teens.option_a: Deadly poison
teens.explanation: James 3:8 — "It is an unruly evil, full of deadly poison."

teens.question: How does James describe the wisdom that is from above?
teens.option_a: Pure, peaceable, gentle, easy to be intreated, full of mercy and good fruits
teens.explanation: James 3:17 — "But the wisdom that is from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, and easy to be intreated, full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality, and without hypocrisy."

adults.question: What does James call the tongue in verse 6?
adults.option_a: A world of iniquity that defileth the whole body and is set on fire of hell
adults.option_b: A lamp that gives light to the body
adults.option_c: A tool for building up the church
adults.option_d: A weapon of warfare against enemies
adults.explanation: James 3:6 — "And the tongue is a fire, a world of iniquity: so is the tongue among our members, that it defileth the whole body, and setteth on fire the course of nature; and it is set on fire of hell."

adults.question: What contradiction does James point out about the tongue?
adults.option_a: With it we bless God and curse men which are made after the similitude of God
adults.option_b: With it we teach truth and learn lies
adults.option_c: With it we sing praises and speak languages
adults.option_d: With it we pray loudly and whisper softly
adults.explanation: James 3:9 — "Therewith bless we God, even the Father; and therewith curse we men, which are made after the similitude of God."

adults.question: What does James say about the fig tree bearing olive berries?
adults.option_a: The fig tree cannot bear olive berries, neither can a vine yield figs
adults.option_b: The fig tree sometimes bears olive berries in good seasons
adults.option_c: The fig tree and olive tree are the same
adults.option_d: Fig trees and vines can bear any fruit
adults.explanation: James 3:12 — "Can the fig tree, my brethren, bear olive berries? either a vine, figs? so can no fountain both yield salt water and fresh."

adults.question: How does James describe wisdom that is not from above?
adults.option_a: Earthly, sensual, devilish
adults.option_b: Heavenly, spiritual, divine
adults.option_c: Simple, humble, gentle
adults.option_d: Powerful, mighty, victorious
adults.explanation: James 3:15 — "This wisdom descendeth not from above, but is earthly, sensual, devilish."

adults.question: Where envying and strife exist, what does James say is found?
adults.option_a: Confusion and every evil work
adults.option_b: Peace and righteousness
adults.option_c: Growth and prosperity
adults.option_d: Wisdom and understanding
adults.explanation: James 3:16 — "For where envying and strife is, there is confusion and every evil work."

adults.question: In what is the fruit of righteousness sown?
adults.option_a: In peace of them that make peace
adults.option_b: In conflict of those who fight for justice
adults.option_c: In solitude of those who meditate
adults.option_d: In power of those who rule
adults.explanation: James 3:18 — "And the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace of them that make peace."

adults.question: Who should show his works with meekness of wisdom?
adults.option_a: A wise man who is endued with knowledge
adults.option_b: A strong warrior who wins battles
adults.option_c: A wealthy merchant who earns profits
adults.option_d: A king who governs his nation
adults.explanation: James 3:13 — "Who is a wise man and endued with knowledge among you? let him shew out of a good conversation his works with meekness of wisdom."

adults.question: What does James say those with bitter envying should not do?
adults.option_a: Glory not and lie not against the truth
adults.option_b: Leave the church immediately
adults.option_c: Fast and pray for three days
adults.option_d: Seek counsel from the elders
adults.explanation: James 3:14 — "But if ye have bitter envying and strife in your hearts, glory not, and lie not against the truth."

kids.question: What does James say to do when the devil comes?
kids.option_a: Resist him and he will flee from you
kids.explanation: James 4:7 — "Resist the devil, and he will flee from you."

kids.question: What happens when you draw near to God?
kids.option_a: He will draw near to you
kids.explanation: James 4:8 — "Draw nigh to God, and he will draw nigh to you."

kids.question: Does James say we know what will happen tomorrow?
kids.option_a: No, we do not know what tomorrow will bring
kids.option_b: Yes, we can predict the future
kids.option_c: Yes, if we pray hard enough
kids.option_d: Only wise people know the future
kids.explanation: James 4:14 — "Whereas ye know not what shall be on the morrow."

teens.question: Where do wars and fightings come from according to James?
teens.option_a: From your lusts that war in your members
teens.explanation: James 4:1 — "From whence come wars and fightings among you? come they not hence, even of your lusts that war in your members?"

teens.question: Why do people not receive what they ask God for?
teens.option_a: Because they ask amiss, that they may consume it upon their lusts
teens.option_b: Because God does not hear them
teens.option_c: Because they did not ask loudly enough
teens.option_d: Because God wants them to figure it out alone
teens.explanation: James 4:3 — "Ye ask, and receive not, because ye ask amiss, that ye may consume it upon your lusts."

teens.question: What does James call friendship with the world?
teens.option_a: Enmity with God
teens.explanation: James 4:4 — "Know ye not that the friendship of the world is enmity with God? whosoever therefore will be a friend of the world is the enemy of God."

teens.question: What does James say our life is like?
teens.option_a: A vapour that appeareth for a little time and then vanisheth away
teens.option_b: A mighty river that flows forever
teens.option_c: A strong mountain that never moves
teens.option_d: A bright star that always shines
teens.explanation: James 4:14 — "For what is your life? It is even a vapour, that appeareth for a little time, and then vanisheth away."

teens.question: What does God give to the humble?
teens.option_a: Grace — God resisteth the proud but giveth grace unto the humble
teens.explanation: James 4:6 — "God resisteth the proud, but giveth grace unto the humble."

adults.question: What does James say the spirit that dwelleth in us lusteth to?
adults.option_a: Envy
adults.option_b: Righteousness
adults.option_c: Charity
adults.option_d: Purity
adults.explanation: James 4:5 — "The spirit that dwelleth in us lusteth to envy."

adults.question: What does James say about speaking evil of a brother?
adults.option_a: He that speaketh evil of his brother speaketh evil of the law and judgeth the law
adults.option_b: It is acceptable if the brother has sinned
adults.option_c: It is only wrong if done in public
adults.option_d: It does not affect one's standing before God
adults.explanation: James 4:11 — "He that speaketh evil of his brother, and judgeth his brother, speaketh evil of the law, and judgeth the law."

adults.question: Who is the one lawgiver who is able to save and to destroy?
adults.option_a: God alone
adults.option_b: Moses
adults.option_c: The high priest
adults.option_d: The king of Israel
adults.explanation: James 4:12 — "There is one lawgiver, who is able to save and to destroy: who art thou that judgest another?"

adults.question: What should believers say instead of boasting about their plans?
adults.option_a: If the Lord will, we shall live, and do this, or that
adults.option_b: We will certainly accomplish our goals
adults.option_c: Our plans will surely succeed
adults.option_d: Nothing can stop our plans
adults.explanation: James 4:15 — "For that ye ought to say, If the Lord will, we shall live, and do this, or that."

adults.question: What does James say about all such rejoicing in one's boastings?
adults.option_a: All such rejoicing is evil
adults.option_b: Such rejoicing is acceptable
adults.option_c: Such rejoicing shows strong faith
adults.option_d: Such rejoicing glorifies God
adults.explanation: James 4:16 — "But now ye rejoice in your boastings: all such rejoicing is evil."

adults.question: What is sin according to James 4:17?
adults.option_a: To him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin
adults.option_b: Only breaking the Ten Commandments
adults.option_c: Only actions that harm other people
adults.option_d: Only things done with evil intent
adults.explanation: James 4:17 — "Therefore to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin."

adults.question: What does James mean by "ye adulterers and adulteresses" in verse 4?
adults.option_a: Spiritual unfaithfulness to God through love of the world
adults.option_b: Literal marital unfaithfulness
adults.option_c: Worshipping in pagan temples
adults.option_d: Breaking business agreements
adults.explanation: James 4:4 — "Ye adulterers and adulteresses, know ye not that the friendship of the world is enmity with God?" This refers to spiritual adultery — unfaithfulness to God.

adults.question: What series of imperatives does James give in verses 7-10 for drawing near to God?
adults.option_a: Submit to God, resist the devil, draw nigh to God, cleanse your hands, purify your hearts, be afflicted, mourn, weep, humble yourselves
adults.option_b: Pray more, give more, attend synagogue, fast weekly
adults.option_c: Study the Torah, memorize the prophets, recite the psalms, teach the children
adults.option_d: Travel to Jerusalem, offer sacrifices, consult the priests, observe the feasts
adults.explanation: James 4:7-10 — "Submit yourselves therefore to God. Resist the devil... Draw nigh to God... Cleanse your hands, ye sinners; and purify your hearts, ye double minded. Be afflicted, and mourn, and weep... Humble yourselves in the sight of the Lord, and he shall lift you up."

kids.question: Who does James tell to be patient, like a farmer waiting for rain?
kids.explanation: James 5:7 — "Be patient therefore, brethren, unto the coming of the Lord. Behold, the husbandman waiteth for the precious fruit of the earth."

kids.question: What should you do if you are sick?
kids.option_a: Call for the elders of the church to pray for you
kids.option_d: Wait and hope it goes away
kids.explanation: James 5:14 — "Is any sick among you? let him call for the elders of the church; and let them pray over him."

kids.question: Whose prayer does James say is very powerful?
kids.option_a: The prayer of a righteous man
kids.option_c: The prayer of the richest person
kids.option_d: The prayer of the oldest person
kids.explanation: James 5:16 — "The effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much."

teens.question: What does James warn the rich about?
teens.option_a: Their riches are corrupted and their gold and silver is cankered
teens.option_b: Their wealth will double in the last days
teens.option_c: Their money will buy them salvation
teens.option_d: Their riches will protect them from judgment
teens.explanation: James 5:2-3 — "Your riches are corrupted, and your garments are motheaten. Your gold and silver is cankered."

teens.question: Who does James use as examples of suffering and patience?
teens.option_a: The prophets who spoke in the name of the Lord, and Job
teens.option_b: The kings and princes of Israel
teens.explanation: James 5:10-11 — "Take, my brethren, the prophets, who have spoken in the name of the Lord, for an example of suffering affliction, and of patience. Ye have heard of the patience of Job."

teens.question: What does James say about swearing oaths?
teens.option_a: Let your yea be yea and your nay, nay
teens.option_b: Swear by heaven for important promises
teens.option_c: Always make oaths to show sincerity
teens.option_d: Oaths are required for all agreements
teens.explanation: James 5:12 — "But above all things, my brethren, swear not, neither by heaven, neither by the earth, neither by any other oath: but let your yea be yea; and your nay, nay."

teens.question: What will the prayer of faith do for the sick?
teens.option_a: Save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up
teens.option_c: Ensure the sick person becomes wealthy
teens.explanation: James 5:15 — "And the prayer of faith shall save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up."

teens.question: How long did Elijah pray for no rain, and it did not rain?
teens.option_a: Three years and six months
teens.explanation: James 5:17 — "He prayed earnestly that it might not rain: and it rained not on the earth by the space of three years and six months."

adults.question: Whose cries have entered into the ears of the Lord of sabaoth?
adults.option_a: The labourers whose hire (wages) was kept back by fraud
adults.option_b: The priests who served in the temple
adults.option_c: The prophets who were rejected
adults.option_d: The widows who lost their husbands
adults.explanation: James 5:4 — "Behold, the hire of the labourers who have reaped down your fields, which is of you kept back by fraud, crieth: and the cries of them which have reaped are entered into the ears of the Lord of sabaoth."

adults.question: How does James describe the lifestyle of the rich in verse 5?
adults.option_a: They lived in pleasure on the earth and been wanton, nourishing their hearts as in a day of slaughter
adults.option_b: They lived humbly and gave to the poor
adults.option_c: They worked harder than anyone else
adults.option_d: They faithfully served God with their wealth
adults.explanation: James 5:5 — "Ye have lived in pleasure on the earth, and been wanton; ye have nourished your hearts, as in a day of slaughter."

adults.question: What does James say about the coming of the Lord?
adults.option_a: The coming of the Lord draweth nigh
adults.option_b: The coming of the Lord is far away
adults.option_c: The coming of the Lord is uncertain
adults.option_d: The coming of the Lord has already happened
adults.explanation: James 5:8 — "Be ye also patient; stablish your hearts: for the coming of the Lord draweth nigh."

adults.question: What end of the Lord does James say we see through Job?
adults.option_a: That the Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy
adults.option_b: That the Lord is harsh and demanding
adults.option_c: That the Lord is distant and uninvolved
adults.option_d: That the Lord tests without purpose
adults.explanation: James 5:11 — "Ye have heard of the patience of Job, and have seen the end of the Lord; that the Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy."

adults.question: What should be done along with prayer for the sick?
adults.option_a: Anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord
adults.option_b: Offering burnt sacrifices at the altar
adults.option_c: Fasting for seven days
adults.option_d: Making a pilgrimage to Jerusalem
adults.explanation: James 5:14 — "Let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord."

adults.question: What connection does James make between confession and healing?
adults.option_a: Confess your faults one to another and pray one for another, that ye may be healed
adults.option_b: Confession is only for the priest to hear
adults.option_c: Healing comes without any confession
adults.option_d: Confession should be private and never shared
adults.explanation: James 5:16 — "Confess your faults one to another, and pray one for another, that ye may be healed."

adults.question: What does James say Elijah was?
adults.option_a: A man subject to like passions as we are
adults.option_b: A supernatural being from heaven
adults.option_c: An angel in human form
adults.option_d: A man without any human weakness
adults.explanation: James 5:17 — "Elias was a man subject to like passions as we are, and he prayed earnestly that it might not rain."

adults.question: What does James say about the one who converts a sinner from the error of his way?
adults.option_a: He shall save a soul from death, and shall hide a multitude of sins
adults.option_b: He shall receive a prophet's reward
adults.option_c: He shall be given authority over ten cities
adults.option_d: He shall be first in the kingdom of heaven
adults.explanation: James 5:20 — "Let him know, that he which converteth the sinner from the error of his way shall save a soul from death, and shall hide a multitude of sins."
